title: The Spark, vol. I no. 14, May 9th 1915

C.S-PARNELL)

Edited by ED. DALTON,

DUBLIN, SUNDAY, MAY oth, 1915.

VIVE VIVIANI!

ON the 13th of September, 1877, the Grand
Masonic Lodge of France by formal act denied the
existence of God. On the 10th of September, in
the following year, it ordered that all symbols of
religion, references to God, and the name of God
should be expunged from its ritual.

These proceedings created much commotion
amongst Masons everywhere.  Masonry is of
English origin, and three-fourths of all the Masons
in the world are resident in Great Britain, the
British Colonies, and the United States of America.
France and Italy come next in Masonic population.
A split ensued. The Masons of Italy, Belgium,
Portugal, Spain, Hungary, and the Balkans sup-
ported the “ Grand Orient ” (the title of the Grand
Masonic Lodge of France), the Masons of the
United States split within themselves—a section
subscribing to the Grand Orient, but a larger sec-
tion refusing to do so. The Masons of Germany
(which contains the smallest number of Masons to
population of any country in Europe except
Austria) repudiated the Grand Orient. So did the
Masons of Ireland (who are a_self-governing body,

and not under the control of the British Masons),

- and finally, after some hesitation, the British Masons
- also repudiated Grand Orientism.

The Grand Orient was, however, undaunted.
It had proclaimed the Grand Secret—¢ There is
no God ”, and it stuck to it, aiming through con-

trol of the Government of France to “drive the

idea of God out of education”. It was bold and’
defiant.  Giosue Carducci composed a “ Hymn to

Satan,” which it performed at its semi-public
functions. The Belgian and Italian Masons wholly
surrendered themselves into its hands,and the cam-
paign “ against the idea of God in education ” was
conducted in Belgium and Italy under the directjon
of France.

The mainstruggle in France between the Grand
Orient Masons and the non or anti-Masons for
political control began in 1898. Clemenceau was
the director of the Grand Orient political press, and
his Private Chief of Staff was a then unknown M.
Viviani, who was at the time only a member of the
second lowest degree of the Grand Orient. The
Governments of Waldeck-Rousseau and Combes,
mere puppet Governments, behind which Clemen-
ceau worked, delivered the great attack, The
military and naval hospitals were laicised, soldiers
were forbidden to enter Catholic clubs, the laws
against the Congregations begun, and then Clemen-
ceau came out into the open and took command.

In the Clemenceau Government M. Viviani
appeared asa Minister. Viviani took active charge
of the operations against the schools, and the last
religious emblems in the schoolrooms' and the last
references to God in the school books were banished.

Price ONe HaLrpENNYY

But Viviani no longer saw why he should play a L

subordinate part to Clemenceau, He was a y,dungé;‘ 3
Clemenceau. found it.

and more energetic man, t
expedient toretire from the Premiership of France,

and in a, little while Viviani occupied his place.

From that exalted seat he boasted that ¢ he had
put out the lights of heaven ”—i.c., banished the

i iame of God from the schools of the country. At
| Pe same time he pained the Premiership he was

hosen one of the Supreme Councillors of the Grand
Drient.

||| To make itself master of the schools of Europe
13 was, however, necessary for the Grand Orient to

n over Great Britain and Germany. Negotiations
vere set on foot for this purpose in 1907. The
| 3ritish Masons refused to come in, but “recognised”
_ he Grand Orient. A mmonty of the German
|| VIasons responded, but the vast majority, led by
| ,he Grand Lodge of Prussia, refused to recognise in
iny way “the French Atheists”. This attitude of
e German Masons led to the bitterest attacks by
lemenceau, who, after their contemptuous refusal
o work with or even recognise the Grand Orient,
'Ecvotcd his journal mainly to encouraging the idea
I>f a Franco-British war against Germany.

ut to return to Ireland

il At this time, for purposes of their own, a section
Ll 5 the Irish Parliamentary Party had rcvwcd the
| J‘mclent Order of Hibernians in Ireland, but could
| ind no footing for it in Dublin. The A O.H., was
| supposed to be a Catholic body, and it was planned
| '0 use their Catholic reputation as a shield for at-
wacking their political opponents and exaltmg them-
clves. Vearious conferences were held in Dublin
Hw’xth the object of establishing what was nicknamed
i at the time in the inner circles of Irish politics “ the
| New Brass Band”. Eventually the persecution of
t]i'c French Church was decided upon as the card to
At a private meeting held in 2 Dublm hotel

vemmcnt, and afterwards to instigate the rchglous
%' dles in Dublm to organise a great protest meeting

\|t, but to use the atmosphere creatcd to establish
| the organization firmly in Dublin.

il In accordance with the plan, a special meeting of
(the Dublin Corporation was called for the 18th of
[anuary, 1907, and the following resolution was
adoptcd —

~“That the Municipal Council of Dublin hereby
tecords its protest against the outrages on religious
'berty in France, and deplores the efforts of the pre-
' sent Government to banish the practices of Christi-
anity from a country for which so many Irishmen
E’:ve fought ‘and died, and that the Right Hon. the
i\ d'Mayqx be requested to forward to the proper
| hormes in France the protest of this Muni-

SPARK

threatened interference of the Government with the
interests of the Irish College in Paris.”

In passing we may say that the resolution was
never forwarded to “ proper authorities” or to any
authorities in France. It was never intended by
those who engineered it that it should.

The demonstration was held in the Pheenix Park
on the succeeding 17th March, On its eve the
¢ Freeman’s Journal > wrote :—

“ To-morrow Ireland’s capital will give strenuous
voice to the almost inexpressible indignation which
its Catholic citizens feel at the infamies which are
being perpetrated in France on that Church of which
it was formerly regarded as the most trusted and
stalwart upholder and defender. That fair land would
still occupy its once proud position in this respect if
it were not for the temporary upperhand which Free-
masons, Atheists, Freethinkers and their sympathisers
have secured in administering their affairs. Through-
out Christendom horror has been expressed at the
legalised crimes that are being all but daily com-
mitted : bishops expelled from their palaces, ecclesi-
astical students ruthlessly evicted from their sgmin-
aries, priests imprisoned for having dared to celebrate
Mass without  the permission of the powers that be.
In fact every conceivable engine of spoliation and
tyranny is being remorselessly employed against the
Church and her heroic ministers.  To-morrow the
Catholics of Dublin will forcxbly proclaim thelr con-
demnation of these terrible acts.”

John Gote, P. J. O’Neill, Robert Keily, and P.
J. Kettle were prominent at this meeting, the most
prominent banner borne at which represented a
Frenchsoldier bayonettinga Cathclic priestaway from
his church door.  No language could exceed the lan-
guage used from the platform against the Govern-
ment of Clemenceau and Viviani—particularly by
Gore and O’Neill, the latter of whom declared that
the French Government was Atheism struggling to
crush Christianity, and that the Irishman who did
not denounce France was a coward. The meeting
adopted the following resolution :—

¢ That, whereas the French Government, in the
course of its war against Christianity in France, has
violated its solemn pledges to the Holy See, has inter-
fered with the rights of conscience, has robbed many
thousands of priests and nuns of their goods and re-
duced them to poverty, and has treated them unjustly
and tyrannically ih many other ways—Resolved, that
we declare that such acts are unworthy of a civilized
and humane Government, and a travesty on its boasted
watchword— Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, and we
call upon all the Catholics of Ireland and Christians
of all denominations, in the name of religion and
humanity, to join with us in our solemn protest.”

p—

I S T SR IR R

b

The sequel took place last week. By an arrange-
ment made between the British Government and
T. P. O’Connor (who is a member, by the way, of
the English Masonic Order) a *“deputation” was
invoked to proceed, in'the name of Ireland, to
France to profess Ireland’s sympathy. The * depu-
tation” was to be entertained by the French Go-
vernment, which was to express friendship for
Ireland and the A.O.H. in Ireland, was to be used
to raise the cry of “Ireland and France” in order
to stimulate recruiting to help—France ! Nugent
was summoned to London and given instructions
how to act. The ‘“deputation” went and was
wined and dined in Paris, The Lord Mayor of
Dublin—head of the body which denounced
Viviani’s Government—was there ; Joe Devlin,
the head of the Catholic A.O.H., was there ; J. D.
Nugent, the Secretary, was there ; and

VIVIANI WAS THERE !

They ate and drank with Viviani, they applauded
Viviani, they rested on the bosom of Viviani, whilst
e, the man who put out * the lights of heaven
—the man who erased the name of God from the
ichool-books of France—spoke, in accordance with
hhe plan, of France’s affection for Ireland !

Viviani, Supreme Councillor of the Grand

Orient, and Devlin ‘and Nugent, heads of the
A.0.H., and Councillor Gallagher, Lord Mayor of
the Christain city of Dublin—what a combination !
How the Orient Masons of the world guffaw with
aughter to find the Irish Catholic organization
nade the tool of their plans | - We have before us
18 we write the resolution adopted by our Dublin
Hibernians in 1907 and published as from Division
57. This is it :—

*“We, the members of Division 67 A.O.H. (Board of
irin), an order which was founded for the purpose of
rotecting the priest duting the dark and stormy days
f our country’s history, and when the price was on
he sagart’s head, protest against the cruel persecution
thich our co-religionist priests and people of the
french nation are at present subject to by aninfidel
Jovernment; but mindful of the promise of our
Jivine Master that the gates of hell shall not prevail
igainst His Church we confidently look to the future
or a reign of glorious peace and prosperity for the
Shurch of France,”

- Now let Division 67 resolute if it dare against
VL. Clemenceau Devlin of the Grand Orient Hib-
ftnians and the never-to-be forgotten J. D, Viviani
Nugent {

THE SPARK 3

THE GREEN KING
With Apelogies to Gilbert and Sullivan.

A king there was, so I am told,
Who reigned uncrowned o'er an island old,
He loved his subjects, young and old,
And they loved—to captivation.
But some there were, a minority mean,
Despicable soreheads all, I wean,
Who, whenever this noble king was seen,
Roared, ““ Here’s sanguine expectation.”
But this I know, and know full well,
When all these died, they went to——well,
Soreheads and faddists fantastical,
Hadve all one destination.

Now this went on, as I am told,
Where the crownless ruled the island old,
And cheered his subjects, in the cold,
With the warmth of expectation.
But his subjects never paid a cent,
For his upkeep, coal, or clothes, or rent,
For all his revenue, it ;was sent,
From cross seas—as a speculation,
For the Yankee ward-boss worked a stunt,
To fetch Irish votes, and he didn’t even grunt,
If the green-backs crossed the pond on a hunt
To help his peculation.

But this old king, so I am told,
The lover of his subjects, young and old,
With the wolf-dog, round-tower, sun-burst bold
And the hearty expectation.
In a sleepy mood, one autumn day,
Bade his subjects—Censor says, ‘‘Don’t say”—
And soon the Yank who found the pay
Let a howl of consternation.
“ We give him dibs to-run the darned show,
And he tries to pack off half the men below,
And reckons we'll forget, if he only lies low,—
Well ! That’s his hallucination,”—M. J. L.
e
VIGILANCE |
I am glad to notice the ceaseless activity of the
Dublin Vigilance Committee. Now, more than
at any previous period, is their existence justified,
when the English press has boldly thrown off the
mask and sought to condone, and even to ‘exalt,
immorality. I notice that our alleged Catholic
Press of Dublin, both the Independent and the Free-
man publications, publish advertisments of the
English advocates of illegitimacy and immorality.
Can no influence be brought to bear on them to
decline such advertisements in the future, or has it
come to this with them, that in selling themselves
to the War Office, they are prepared to barter the
religious reputation of Ireland.

"~ COLLEGE GREEN DIVISION

i The rumourists are busy juggling with the names
~ of alleged candidates for above vacancy. The name
~ of Roger Casement has been circulated by some
enterprising penny-a-liner, but the report has been
contradicted with alacrity, and possibly by the same
~ pen that originated it, which is not an uncommon
~ practice amongst “journalists,” who are always
keenly on the scent to make a bit both ways.

Of course Casement’s name will not be submitted
to the electorate. In the first place it would in-
 volve expense without compensating value, and
- secondly, supporters of Casement would be liable
 for breach of the ¢ Defence of the Realm Act”.
.~ But if any other decent man’s name is put forward
1 trust readers of THE SPARK in the division will
~ vote for him, not, indeed, that he may be returned

to Westminster because that I would consider a
doubtful honour to the candidate himself and a ser-
vice of little value to College Green ; but that the
nominee of the perverts and political soupers may
be defeated, and a £400 dab administered to the
_party in the solar plexus.
| Johnnie Ronayne and Mrs. Gallagher are run-
~ ning the Lord Mayor for the seat. Between the
two L pity poor Jimmie, who isn’t really an ambi-
tious man, and probably would be more at homein
~his own back yard.  Jimmie avic, did you ever
hear of Humpty Dumpty ?
Lorcan, the ex-Lord Mayor, is mentioned, also
. the Maw Coghlan, whose brother is half-secretary
- to the Lord Mayor, and, of course, the ubiquitous
~ J. D. Nugent, the originator of the ¢ German gold”
canard, the man who never took part in any move-
ment without first assuring himself that there was
coin in the coffers, because patriotism * which
doesn't pay is only faction and crankery according
to the great Hib. Nugent.
_..:‘.:.—__

TRINITY WARD ELECTION

Twao candidates have been nominated for the
Councillorship of ‘the above ward rendered vacant
through Mr. O’Toole’s election as Alderman.
They are Miss Wheeler and Mr. Thos. Foran.
Under normal circumstances I would support the
- lady for the vacancy, but circumstances are not
- normal. The lady is the candidate of the reaction-
aries, of the corrupt politicians, and of 'the food
- adulterators.  They will support her to a man and
place the local party machine at her service. . Her
election means the perpetuation of the rotten sys-
- tem of Municipal Government to which we have

THE SPARK

become accustomed ; it means the postponement of
the day of reform in our civil life. Many well-
disposed persons will vote for Miss Wheeler, not
because they approve of her politics or of her social
programme, but in order to prevent the return of her
opponent, Mr: Foran, who is, I am informed, a
Socialist.

Well I am not a Socialist, neither do I fear
Socialism, Socialism is, I think, purely a system of
economics. I do not believe that our modern social
evils are to be remedied by economic changes solely.
But I have more fear of corrupt iand hypocritical
Hibernianism that of Socialism. I am seriously afriad
of the men who traffic in the name of religion, but
who fail to comply even with the bare letter of the
religious law. Men who have God’s name on their
tongue, but the spirit of evil, of cunning, and of sel-
fishnessiin their hearts. I would vote forany Sacial-
ist in preference to such men, because Socialism is
really not a danger to Ireland at all. The people
who are afraid of it are afraid either through ignor=
ance or because they unjustly possess property of
which Socialism would deprive them.  As far as
Socialism in Ireland is concerned I regard it as a
revolt against existing evils rather than as the

serious and deliberate remedy advanced by earnest.

men and women for the relief of those evils, which
I again maintain are not mainly economic at all.

Holding those views, I recommend the candida-
ture of Mr. Foran for Trinity Ward. There is
less danger to faith and morals in electing an oppon-
ent of corruption and jobbery in public life, even
though he be a Socialist, than a lady not a Socialist,
but who is backed by the influences which create
Socialism and irreligion.

_..—Q:...'_._

I am informed that Cumann na mBan (Dublin
Central Branch) have formed a publication com-
mittee which intends issuing, at regular and fre-
quent intervals, 2 series of pamphlets dealing with
various phases of Irish Nationalism. = They will
sell at one penny each, and No. 1 will be on sale
at the newsagents on’ Wednesday next. ' I com-
mend these pamphlets to readers of THE SPARK.
Buy them and pass them on. The ¢ Khaki *
rulers of Ireland have decided, with the acquiescence
of the Redmondite Perverts, that the vdice, of
Nationalist Ireland must be hushed. The new
enterprise of Cumann na mBan will show ' them
that Irish Nationalism is still articulate,
great conspiracy, to strangle it.
